title: Backend Security Review Handoff
scope: backend-focused security review with related frontend trust-boundary findings

summary.primary_theme: authentication and authorization boundaries are weak or collapse under missing config, and several endpoints rely on exposed client-side API keys plus unowned session identifiers
recommended_execution_order: Block forged auth and public admin/log access
Add ownership verification for session-bound resources
Fix OAuth state and session-link trust model
Reduce token exposure and harden long-term auth storage

priorities.title: JWT secret fallback allows token forgery if env is missing
priorities.risk: If JWT_SECRET is omitted in any deployed environment, an attacker can mint valid tokens and impersonate arbitrary users.

evidence.note: JWT secret falls back to a known static string.
Same secret is used for encode/decode.

standard_remediation: Fail fast at startup if JWT_SECRET is missing in non-local environments.
Remove insecure default secret entirely.
Optionally validate secret length/entropy at boot.
Rotate any existing signing secret after patching if there is any chance fallback was ever used.
implementation_notes: Preferred pattern: explicit config validation during app startup, not lazy fallback.
If local developer convenience is needed, gate a dev-only fallback behind an explicit ENV such as APP_ENV=local and never as the default path.
acceptance_criteria: Server refuses to start when JWT_SECRET is absent outside local dev.
Known fallback string no longer exists in code.
Existing tests cover missing-secret startup behavior.

priorities.title: Client-exposed API key means protected endpoints are effectively public
priorities.risk: Endpoints guarded by X-API-Key are callable by any browser user because the key is shipped in frontend build variables.

evidence.note: API protection depends only on API_SECRET_KEY.
VITE_API_KEY is attached to requests from browser.
Checklist endpoints use same exposed API key.

standard_remediation: Do not use browser-exposed build variables as backend shared secrets.
For user-facing routes, replace shared API-key auth with user/session auth or make endpoint intentionally public with narrower response scope.
For admin/log routes, require authenticated admin authorization or move behind trusted network/gateway auth.
For machine-to-machine protection, keep secrets only on server-to-server paths.
implementation_notes: Treat all endpoints currently relying on VITE_API_KEY as unauthenticated from a threat-model standpoint.
A good immediate containment option is to remove frontend access to admin/log APIs and gate them with role-checked JWT or infra auth.
acceptance_criteria: No frontend bundle contains a backend-shared secret.
Log export and log listing endpoints are inaccessible to normal site visitors.
User-facing endpoints use identity-based auth or are intentionally public and sanitized.

priorities.title: Session-bound resources lack ownership enforcement
priorities.risk: Anyone who knows or guesses a valid session_id can read or mutate another session's report/checklist data.

evidence.note: Only checks session existence, not ownership.
Read/write checklist is allowed when session exists.
Frontend fetches report by raw session_id for anonymous flows.

standard_remediation: Introduce a server-side ownership model for session-bound resources.
Require authenticated user ownership for private report/history/checklist access wherever feasible.
If anonymous sessions must exist, bind them to an unguessable server-issued bearer capability or signed cookie, not just a raw session_id string.
Reject requests when caller cannot prove ownership of the session.
implementation_notes: Short-term: disable anonymous read/write access for report/checklist endpoints unless the product explicitly requires it.
Long-term: replace plain session_id trust with signed opaque session tokens or same-site HttpOnly cookies.
acceptance_criteria: Session existence alone is no longer sufficient to read or mutate session data.
Anonymous flows, if retained, use proof of possession beyond raw session_id.

priorities.title: Session linking endpoint can attach arbitrary session_id to logged-in user
priorities.risk: An attacker can associate someone else's anonymous session with their own account if they obtain the session_id.

evidence.note: link-session trusts provided session_id without ownership proof.
Session user_id is overwritten if session exists.
Frontend submits locally stored session_id directly.

standard_remediation: Require proof that the caller initiated or possesses the anonymous session before linking.
Do not allow reassignment of already-owned sessions without stronger authorization logic.
Prefer server-issued session cookies or signed one-time link tokens over client-submitted bare identifiers.
implementation_notes: Good standard pattern: anonymous session cookie is upgraded in place after login, instead of a public link endpoint taking arbitrary IDs.
At minimum, reject linking if session already has a different user_id.
acceptance_criteria: Cross-user or arbitrary session linking is denied.
Session linking requires cryptographic/session proof, not only knowledge of the ID.
Regression tests cover re-link attempts and stolen session_id scenarios.

priorities.title: Google OAuth flow lacks state parameter validation
priorities.risk: The login flow is vulnerable to login CSRF/account confusion because authorization requests are not bound to the initiating browser state.

evidence.note: OAuth initiation does not generate or include state.
Callback validates code only; no state verification.

standard_remediation: Generate a high-entropy state value at OAuth start.
Store it in a secure, same-site cookie or server-side session.
Verify exact match on callback before exchanging the code.
Optionally include PKCE as an additional defense if the OAuth client architecture benefits from it.
implementation_notes: State is mandatory even in server-side OAuth flows.
If redirect targets differ by environment, ensure state validation is environment-agnostic and not tied to fragile host assumptions.
acceptance_criteria: OAuth callback fails when state is missing or mismatched.
State is generated per auth attempt and cleared after use.
Automated tests cover missing and invalid state.

priorities.title: Long-lived JWT is stored in localStorage
priorities.risk: Any XSS in the frontend can exfiltrate a 30-day bearer token and fully impersonate the user.

evidence.note: Token is stored client-side immediately after login.

standard_remediation: Prefer HttpOnly, Secure, SameSite cookies for session/auth tokens.
Reduce token lifetime and add refresh-token rotation if needed.
If bearer tokens must stay in JS space, minimize lifetime and harden CSP/XSS posture.
implementation_notes: This is not the first fix to land, but it should follow once the core auth boundary issues are stabilized.
Cookie-based auth will also simplify session ownership and OAuth callback handling.
acceptance_criteria: Primary auth token is no longer readable from JavaScript, or lifetime is substantially reduced with compensating controls.
Logout and session renewal flows are updated accordingly.

quick_wins.action: Remove JWT fallback secret and make startup fail when JWT_SECRET is absent outside local dev.
Disable or heavily gate /api/v1/logs, /api/v1/logs/export, /api/v1/logs/users, and any admin-style stats endpoints until real authz is in place.
Stop treating VITE_API_KEY as a secret. Remove it from browser auth model.
Temporarily require authenticated ownership for /api/report/* and /api/checklist/* or disable anonymous access.
Block linking of sessions that already belong to another user and add ownership proof to link flow.
Add OAuth state generation and callback verification.

goals: Eliminate forged auth by config hardening
Shut down public access to sensitive operational endpoints
Replace arbitrary session linking with session proof or cookie upgrade
Move away from localStorage bearer model
Normalize private vs public endpoint policy

test_recommendations: Add tests that startup/config validation fails when JWT_SECRET is missing in production-like mode.
Add authz tests proving one user cannot read another user's report/checklist/history.
Add tests for session linking refusal when session belongs to a different user or lacks ownership proof.
Add OAuth tests for missing, invalid, and valid state handling.
Add endpoint tests showing admin/log routes reject anonymous and non-admin callers.
notes_for_claude_code: Prioritize real risk reduction over preserving current anonymous UX.
Treat any path depending on browser-provided X-API-Key as already exposed.
If product requires anonymous sessions, use a signed server-issued capability or secure cookie rather than trusting raw session_id.
Avoid partial fixes that leave session ownership inconsistent across report, checklist, logs, and session-link endpoints.
